meta.name: AI-Driven Platform Engineering — Living Memory Graph
meta.description: A curated, PR-extensible knowledge graph of the concepts, decisions, patterns, threats, and tools from the book. Connect a local MCP-native agent and query it. Grows over time via pull requests.

nodes.title: Platform as a Product
nodes.summary: Treat the internal platform as a product with real users (developers) who deserve a great experience.
nodes.body: Platform engineering moved complexity rather than removing it. Treating the platform as a product—with a roadmap, users, and a value story—is how teams stop it becoming a cost center. This is the promise the whole book pays off.

nodes.title: Developer cognitive load
nodes.summary: The mental weight developers carry fighting infrastructure and tooling instead of building features.
nodes.body: The central pain the book attacks. AI-driven platforms exist to lift this load: anticipate needs, hide complexity, make the paved road effortless.

nodes.title: DORA metrics / SLO / SLI
nodes.summary: Measure what matters—deployment frequency, lead time, change fail rate, MTTR—and tie platform performance to business value.
nodes.body: Go beyond vanity metrics; connect platform outcomes to revenue and reliability. Foundation for later chapters where AI reports these on demand.

nodes.title: AIOps
nodes.summary: Applying ML to operations (monitoring, incident correlation, anomaly detection); the seed that grew into AI-driven platform engineering.
nodes.body: Coined by Gartner mid-2010s to fight alert fatigue. Proved AI could spot patterns invisible to humans, motivating a smarter way to manage complexity.

nodes.title: Build vs. buy AI platform capability
nodes.summary: Weigh customization/control against time-to-market when adding AI to the developer portal.
nodes.body: Custom builds maximize control but cost time; existing platforms accelerate but constrain. A recurring strategic crossroads for platform teams.

nodes.title: Large Language Models
nodes.summary: Token-predicting Transformer models that interpret intent and generate standards-aligned output.
nodes.body: The foundation of generative AI platforms. Translate messy natural-language requests into IaC, docs, and decisions. Default to the latest, most capable models.

nodes.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
nodes.summary: Ground model output in retrieved organizational knowledge to answer 'how was this solved before?'
nodes.body: Answers from your own corpus rather than the model's frozen weights. In Ch4 it becomes a local Postgres+pgvector pipeline over platform knowledge.

nodes.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
nodes.summary: A standard protocol for exposing tools/resources to AI hosts—answers 'what is true right now?'
nodes.body: Where RAG recalls the past, MCP queries live state. The book's default integration surface for agents; the memory graph itself is served over MCP.

nodes.title: Prompt vs RAG vs Agents vs Fine-tune vs Train
nodes.summary: Choose the lightest customization that meets the need; escalate only when justified.
nodes.body: A spectrum of cost and control. Most platform use cases are solved with prompting + RAG + tools long before fine-tuning is warranted.

nodes.title: Context in AI models
nodes.summary: Single-shot vs few-shot and the strategies for feeding the right context into platform operations.
nodes.body: The raw material of good AI behavior. Leads to organizational-knowledge systems and, eventually, context-as-a-module.

nodes.title: Organizational knowledge systems
nodes.summary: Standardized, reusable knowledge structures aligned with teams—context providers and consumers.
nodes.body: Turns tacit, tribal knowledge into structured, reusable context. The direct ancestor of the living memory graph.

nodes.title: Conversational interfaces
nodes.summary: Developers express intent in natural language; the platform produces standards-aligned artifacts.
nodes.body: The user-facing equivalent of a Kubernetes Operator: receive intent, apply knowledge, produce structure. Backstage + MCP make it real.

nodes.title: Intent as an abstraction
nodes.summary: Describe the desired state; let the system reconcile it—the turning point from step-by-step to declarative.
nodes.body: Operators/CRDs proved intent is powerful: once the platform understands what you want, it automates the rest. Forward Engineering extends this to whole workflows.

nodes.title: Agentic harness
nodes.summary: Just an architecture: a loop + memory + tools + guardrails + orchestration wrapped around a model.
nodes.body: Demystifies agentic AI. Once seen as architecture (not magic) you can diagram, version, swap the model, and reason about failure modes like any distributed system. Built across Ch6–7.

nodes.title: Workflow vs Agent: a spectrum of determinism
nodes.summary: From fully-scripted workflows to open-ended agents; choose the least autonomy that solves the problem.
nodes.body: Map the workflow before building the agent. Determinism where you can, autonomy where you must.

nodes.title: Anatomy of an agent: memory, tools, scheduling
nodes.summary: Short/long-term memory, tools-as-governed-APIs, and self-invocation/scheduling.
nodes.body: The pillars that turn a text generator into an operator. Tools are APIs with governance at multiple layers.

nodes.title: Personal vs production agents
nodes.summary: A personal helper and a production operator have very different bars for governance and reliability.
nodes.body: Production agents earn autonomy through the same controls humans face: GitOps, review, audit.

nodes.title: Operation agents propose via GitOps
nodes.summary: Production agents propose change through PR → merge → ArgoCD, never kubectl apply by hand.
nodes.body: The agent is held to the same deploy channel as humans. Reconciliation and review stay in the loop; the agent gains leverage without bypassing control.

nodes.title: Human-in-the-loop control
nodes.summary: High-impact actions require validation/approval; AI assists decisions rather than replacing them.
nodes.body: Layered by risk: automate low-risk tasks, gate production changes. The brake that lets you go fast safely.

nodes.title: Model guardrails
nodes.summary: Input/output/policy/execution guardrails—the rumble strips that keep AI within organizational boundaries.
nodes.body: Adaptive: strict where risk is high, flexible where low. Extends existing platform guardrails (templates, modules, policy-as-code) to generated text and autonomous decisions.

nodes.title: Identity propagation
nodes.summary: Carry the user's identity through every AI step; act on delegated permissions, never a shared privileged token.
nodes.body: Solves 'the bot-account did it.' JWT claims (role, team, environments, tenant_id) enforce RBAC and tenant isolation at execution time.

nodes.title: Prompt injection
nodes.summary: Malicious instructions that hijack the model's reasoning; severity explodes once tools can act.
nodes.body: Targets reasoning, not parsers. With tool access an attacker influences what the system does, not just what it says.

nodes.title: Memory poisoning
nodes.summary: Injecting misleading data into memory/retrieval so the agent later relies on corrupted context.
nodes.body: Affects long-term memory, vector stores, runbooks, caches. Directly motivates provenance and signing for shared/curated context (see downloadable brains).

nodes.title: Compliance as a platform capability
nodes.summary: Embed GDPR/SOC2/etc. controls into Git workflows, policy-as-code, and approval pipelines.
nodes.body: Turns audit from a manual burden into continuous, built-in oversight. Explainability and auditability of every prompt/context/tool call.

nodes.title: Forward Engineering
nodes.summary: Amazon inverting 'working backwards': start with a prototype, write the aligning doc last—but only works when intent/specs are explicit.
nodes.body: AWS's Swami Sivasubramanian noted it's now easier to build a working demo than write the classic PRFAQ, so teams start with the prototype. The catch that anchors the book's thesis: agents fail without explicit specs, tests, and edge cases. Not to be confused with AWS 'Forward Deployed Engineering.'

nodes.title: Governance-as-a-module
nodes.summary: Package policy like a Helm chart: composable, versioned, PR-updated—enforced wherever it lands.
nodes.body: Governance stops being prose in a wiki and becomes a dependency you can see in your manifest. Modularized in parallel with infrastructure.

nodes.title: Context-as-a-module
nodes.summary: Package the context an agent needs—versioned, composable, shareable—like an infrastructure module.
nodes.body: The logical continuation of organizational-knowledge systems. Directly enables the memory graph and, at the extreme, downloadable brains.

nodes.title: Living memory graph
nodes.summary: A curated, PR-extensible knowledge graph served over MCP that a reader's own agent connects to.
nodes.body: This artifact. A working appendix that keeps teaching after the last page. Typed nodes and edges the authors keep extending; the concrete, shippable form of context-as-a-module.

nodes.title: Downloadable brains (curated context as a tradeable good)
nodes.summary: People will sell 'brains'—nothing more than curated context—that you download into an agent, and eventually a robot.
nodes.body: The capstone bet and logical extreme of context-as-a-module. Credibility depends on provenance and signing (a 'lemon market' without them), tying straight back to memory-poisoning defenses.

nodes.title: The up-leveled organization
nodes.summary: As platforms make the implicit explicit and agents execute it, ambiguity is exposed and leverage compounds.
nodes.body: What fails is undocumented/undecided process, not technology. Business and technology mix; exposure is a diagnosis, not a punishment. The one-person, high-leverage business becomes conceivable.
